His 1936 birth falls after Utah began systematic vital registration (1898), so a birth certificate may exist in state archives; the 1940 census would show him at age 4 in a household with parents; his 2023 obituary may name surviving family or parents; and his SSA application may name his parents. These record types provide the most direct evidence for parentage.

entry.rationale: The 1940 U.S. Census is fully public and would enumerate Alan Glen Applegarth at age 4 in his parents' household, directly naming his parents, their ages, birthplaces, and relationship to him. This is the highest-probability source for direct parentage evidence given his birth year.

Utah vital registration began in 1898; a birth certificate for Alan Glen Applegarth (born 29 April 1936, Ogden, Weber County) would name both parents and the mother's maiden name. The FamilySearch 'Utah, Births and Christenings, 1892-1941' (coll. 1675542) covers this period and is indexed.

Alan Glen Applegarth died 16 May 2023 in Providence, Cache County, Utah. His obituary, published in Utah newspapers, would very likely name his parents (or note them as deceased), his spouse Carole Sue Checketts, and siblings. 'Utah, Obituaries from Utah Newspapers, 1850-2005' (coll. 2302011) covers Utah newspapers; also check Ancestry/Newspapers.com for 2023 obituaries.

The parents' marriage record (civil or church) would confirm the couple as a unit and supply the mother's maiden name — a critical datum that census and death records often omit. The 'Utah, Weber County Marriages, 1887-1941' (coll. 2291514) and 'Utah, County Marriages, 1871-1941' (coll. 1803977) are indexed on FamilySearch for the likely marriage period. This item is dedicated to the parents' marriage, not Alan's own.

Alan Glen Applegarth's Social Security Death Index entry and/or SS-5 application (Form SS-5) may name his parents. The SS-5 application asks for the applicant's full name, address, employer, and parents' full names (including mother's maiden name). FamilySearch indexes the SSDI; the SS-5 images are available through Ancestry or the SSA.

If Alan's father was born c. 1895-1920, he would have registered for the WWII draft (1940-1947). FamilySearch 'Utah, World War II Draft Registration Cards, 1940-1947' (coll. 2659402) is indexed and would confirm the father's identity, age, and address in Ogden — corroborating co-residence with Alan's family.

Death certificates for Alan's parents (if they predeceased 1966) would appear in 'Utah, Death Certificates, 1904-1966' (coll. 1747615). A parent's death certificate names the deceased, may give parents (grandparents of Alan), and names informants. If parents died after 1966, Utah State Archives records may apply.

The 1930 U.S. Census (6 years before Alan's birth) would enumerate his parents' household prior to his birth, confirming their identities, ages, and birthplaces. If older siblings are present, their birth years and places help triangulate the family's residential history. Useful corroboration for parents identified in the 1940 census.

The LDS Church Census (src_002) was recorded by an LDS stake clerk enumerating households in North Weber Utah Stake in 1940 — a different institution, time, and informant than the other two. The Weber County marriage record (src_003) was created by the county clerk at the moment of the 1926 marriage ceremony, with the bride as informant. The GenealogyBank obituary (src_001) was submitted by family members in 2023. No source derives from another: each rests on its own chain of custody and informant. Two independent sources (different record types, different decades, different institutions) agree on 'Harvey'; one gives 'Hardy.'
entry.weighing_analysis: The decisive factors are informant proximity and contemporaneity. The marriage record (a_019) is the strongest: Olive Harvey herself reported her maiden name when she married Jack Applegarth in 1926 — a contemporaneous self-report at the moment her maiden name was most salient and legally recorded. The obituary (a_007) reinforces this: the 2023 family submitter gave 'Olive Harvey Annie Applegarth,' consistent with the marriage record 97 years earlier; families reliably preserve maiden names through documents and oral tradition. Against these two independent confirmations, the church census (a_015) — recorded by a ward clerk collecting household data orally in 1940, 14 years after the marriage — stands alone with 'Hardy.' The phonetic similarity between 'Hardy' and 'Harvey' (identical syllable structure; medial consonant v vs. d; easily blurred in spoken dictation) makes aural mishearing or pen-slip the most parsimonious explanation.
entry.resolution_rationale: The dispute concerns Olive's maiden surname: 'Hardy' (LDS Church Census, 1940) versus 'Harvey' (Weber County marriage record, 1926; GenealogyBank obituary, 2023). The distinction matters because the maiden name is required to distinguish Olive from other women of similar name and to identify her family of origin. The marriage record names the bride as 'Olive Harvey' — a primary-quality record in which the bride herself provided her own name at a legal ceremony. The obituary names 'Olive Harvey Annie Applegarth' as Alan's mother, consistent with the marriage record. The church census gives 'Olive Annie Hardy Applegarth,' recorded 14 years after the marriage by a stake clerk who likely collected the information orally from a household member. 'Harvey' is the more reliable form: it is corroborated by two independent record types, and the marriage record — in which Olive was the informant about her own name — carries primary quality for this specific fact. 'Hardy' in the church census fits the well-documented transcription-error pattern caused by phonetic similarity: 'Hardy' and 'Harvey' differ only in the medial consonant (d vs. v), a distinction easily lost in oral dictation or rapid handwriting. An LDS ward clerk writing from Olive or her husband's spoken answer in a 1940 household visit could plausibly hear 'Harvey' and write 'Hardy.' The maiden name is Harvey.

entry.description: Alan Glen Applegarth's birth year: LDS Church Census (src_005/a_028) gives 1935; GenealogyBank obituary (src_001/a_002) gives 29 April 1936.
entry.independence_analysis: The two assertions come from independent sources. The LDS Church Census (src_005) was compiled by a church stake enumerator in 1940 from a household member's oral report — different institution, time, and informant than the obituary. The GenealogyBank obituary (src_001) was submitted by family members in 2023, 83 years after the census and presumably drawing on documentary evidence (birth certificate, patriarchal blessing, or official family records). Neither source derives from the other; the informant chains are distinct.
entry.weighing_analysis: The decisive factors are precision and informant basis. The obituary (a_002) gives an exact date — 29 April 1936 — with day, month, and year specificity that strongly suggests the family consulted a documentary source (birth certificate or equivalent). The church census (a_028) gives only a birth year of 1935, almost certainly calculated by the stake clerk from an age reported orally by a parent: in 1940, Alan was 3 years old before 29 April and 4 years old after. If the census was conducted in the first quarter of 1940 and a parent said 'four' (anticipating the April birthday), the clerk's subtraction 1940 − 5 = 1935 could follow from treating 'four' as 'going on five.' This one-year off-by-one error is the most common artifact in age-based census records. The exact date in the obituary, consistent with the research objective's established birth date, is materially more reliable than the computed census year.
entry.resolution_rationale: The dispute concerns Alan Glen Applegarth's birth year: 1935 (LDS Church Census, 1940) versus 29 April 1936 (GenealogyBank obituary, 2023). The distinction matters because it affects which household and census records belong to Alan and, more directly, the documentary coherence of his life record. The 2023 obituary gives 29 April 1936 as an exact birth date — day-level precision that indicates the family had access to an official or ecclesiastical record (birth certificate, patriarchal blessing, LDS record of birth) when submitting the obituary. The LDS Church Census gives only birth year 1935, which was almost certainly computed by the stake enumerator from a parent's oral age report: before Alan's April 29 birthday in 1940 he was 3 years old, and a parent rounding up to 'four' combined with the clerk subtracting from 1940 yields 1940 − 5 = 1935. The exact date from the obituary is more reliable because documentary precision at the day level is a reliable marker of record-based reporting rather than recollection; the 1935 census year is a classic off-by-one calculation artifact. This error pattern — census ages reported as the nearest whole year producing computed birth-year discrepancies of ±1–2 years — is one of the most thoroughly documented inaccuracies in American census research. Alan Glen Applegarth's birth year is 1936.

## Parentage of Alan Glen Applegarth (Born 29 April 1936, Ogden, Weber County, Utah)

**Conclusion (Probable):** The parents of Alan Glen Applegarth were **John O'Brien Applegarth** (born about 1905, Llewellyn[?], Nebraska) and **Olive Harvey Applegarth** (born about 1906, Kaysville, Davis County, Utah; maiden name Harvey).

### Evidence Summary

Three independent sources from different record types, institutions, and decades establish this parentage.

**1. GenealogyBank Obituary, 2023 (derivative; family informant; secondary/direct)**

Alan Glen Applegarth's published obituary, accessed via FamilySearch (ark:/61903/1:1:6239-LQSN),[S1] explicitly names John O'Brien Applegarth as Alan's father and Olive Harvey Annie Applegarth as Alan's mother. The informant was an unidentified family member who submitted the obituary approximately four days after Alan's 16 May 2023 death. This is a derivative source — a FamilySearch/GenealogyBank index entry abstracting from the published newspaper text — with secondary information quality for the parentage facts (the family reported parentage from knowledge, not from firsthand witness to the births). The full obituary text and original newspaper were not examined. Surviving close family is nevertheless among the most reliable informant classes for the parentage of a person born in the twentieth century, and the names provided are specific.

**2. LDS Church Census, North Weber Utah Stake, 1940 (derivative; church enumerator; primary/direct for residence)**

The 1940 LDS Church Census enumerated John O'Brien Applegarth (born 1905, Nebraska; ark:/61903/1:1:8778-JZ3Z),[S4] Olive Annie Applegarth (born 1906, Kaysville, Utah; ark:/61903/1:1:8778-JZW2),[S2] and Alan Glenn Applegarth (born 1935/1936, Ogden, Utah; ark:/61903/1:1:8778-JZ6Z)[S5] together in the same stake household. The household co-residence at Alan's age of 3–4 corroborates the parent-child relationships named in the obituary. All three entries are derivative FamilySearch database records; the underlying physical census registers were not examined.

**3. Weber County Civil Marriage Record, 1926 (derivative; county clerk; primary/direct for marriage event)**

A civil marriage record in Weber County, Utah, records the marriage of Jack Applegarth to Olive Harvey on 20 October 1926 (ark:/61903/1:1:QK6K-4FCD).[S3] "Jack" is a standard established nickname for "John"; the groom's birth year of about 1905 matches John O'Brien Applegarth's birth year as given in the church census. The bride reported her maiden name as Harvey at the time of the ceremony — primary-quality information for her own name. The marriage in 1926 predates Alan's 1936 birth by ten years, establishing the couple in the correct time and place.

### Conflict Resolution

**Maiden surname — Hardy vs. Harvey (c_001, resolved):** The LDS Church Census renders Olive's maiden name as "Hardy"; the marriage record and the obituary both give "Harvey." The marriage record is authoritative: Olive Harvey self-reported her maiden name in a legal ceremony in 1926 — the most contemporaneous and proximate source for this fact. "Hardy" and "Harvey" are phonetically near-identical, and the church census record is best explained as an aural transcription error by the ward enumerator. The maiden name is **Harvey**.

**Alan's birth year — 1935 vs. 1936 (c_002, resolved):** The LDS Church Census gives a calculated birth year of 1935; the obituary gives the exact date 29 April 1936. The obituary's day-level precision indicates the family consulted a documentary source (birth certificate or LDS record). The census year is computed from a reported age subject to the well-documented ±1-year off-by-one artifact in census records when enumeration precedes the subject's birthday. Alan Glen Applegarth was born **29 April 1936**.

### Source Limitations and Tier Rationale

All five sources accessed are derivative indexed databases; no original record images were examined. Alan's 1936 Utah birth certificate — the most definitive original record for this parentage question — is sealed under Utah's approximately 100-year privacy rule and is not publicly accessible. These limitations prevent a "proved" conclusion. The preponderance of three fully independent derivative sources with convergent, uncontradicted evidence supports a **probable** conclusion. Tier advancement to "proved" would require access to at least one original record with primary parentage information, most plausibly Alan's sealed birth certificate (available to direct heirs on request) or the original LDS Church Census register images.

### Conclusion

With **probable** confidence: **John O'Brien Applegarth** (born about 1905, Nebraska) and **Olive Harvey Annie Applegarth** (born about 1906, Kaysville, Utah; maiden name Harvey) were the parents of Alan Glen Applegarth, born 29 April 1936 in Ogden, Weber County, Utah. No contradicting evidence was identified in any examined source.
